notes: USAspending's own 'Unreported Data' line, FY2025 - the platform's built-in admission of what agencies did not report to it. Includes but is not limited to classified activity. FLAGSHIP CLAIM.
public_note: The tracking site keeps a line called "Unreported Data" — money agencies never told it about. Secret programs are part of this line, but not all of it.

public_note: What the Defense Department says it owns. Its auditors could not verify the books behind this figure.

notes: 8th consecutive disclaimer of opinion (FY2018-FY2025). 26 material weaknesses, 2 significant deficiencies. F-35 Global Spares Pool assets unverifiable -> material misstatement on Agency-Wide statements. DoD is the only major federal agency never to pass an audit. Clean-audit mandate: Dec 31 2028 (NDAA FY2024 sec.1005).
public_note: Failed, for the eighth year in a row. Auditors found 26 serious accounting problems and could not give an opinion on the books at all. It is the only major federal agency never to pass an audit.

notes: FY2013 Congressional Budget Justification ('black budget'), Snowden disclosure, Washington Post 2013-08-29. Only full internal NIP breakdown ever public. Basis for all ratio-persistence models.
public_note: In 2013, a leaked internal document showed — for the first and only time — how the intelligence budget divides between agencies.

notes: CIA share of FY2013 NIP request: 28%. Largest single agency.
public_note: The CIA's share: about 28% of the intelligence budget, the largest of any agency.

notes: NSA share of FY2013 NIP request: ~21%.
public_note: The NSA's share: about 21%.

notes: NRO share of FY2013 NIP request: ~20%.
public_note: The National Reconnaissance Office — the spy-satellite agency: about 20%.

notes: NGA share of FY2013 NIP request.

notes: General Defense Intelligence Program (DIA) share of FY2013 NIP request.

notes: MODELED: CIA FY2013 share (28% of NIP) projected onto FY2025 appropriated NIP $73.3B = ~$20.5B point, +/-15% drift band for 12 years of unknown reallocation. Assumptions in METHODOLOGY 4.1. Never sum with T1 claims.
public_note: An estimate, clearly labeled as one: if the CIA still holds roughly its 2013 share, its budget today falls between $18 and $24 billion. Twelve years of unknown change is why this is a range, not a number.

notes: DoD OIG DODIG-2016-113: $6.5T in unsupported journal-entry adjustments in Army General Fund FY2015 statements. CRITICAL FRAMING: gross accounting adjustments (same dollars re-booked repeatedly, both directions), NOT missing cash. Evidence of unauditable books, not a spending figure. Quarantined from all sums. See METHODOLOGY 5.
public_note: In 2015, Pentagon inspectors found $6.5 trillion in accounting entries the Army could not support. This is the same dollars re-entered over and over — proof the books can't be audited, not money that vanished. It is never added to anything on this site.

notes: COMPUTED: C-0001 minus C-0005 = $1.711T of FY2025 outlays not visible as award obligations. Mostly explainable (interest ~$880B, federal compensation, non-award ops) - the build must decompose this before presenting. Disclosed IC topline C-0013 sits INSIDE this residual.
public_note: Total spending minus traceable spending: $1.7 trillion the tracking can't show. Most of it has ordinary explanations — debt interest, salaries — and this site's job is to account for those before saying anything about the rest.

notes: CRS R44381: IC spending has held ~11% of national defense spending since 2007. Sanity-check model for any projected agency figure.
public_note: For nearly twenty years, intelligence has held steady at about 11% of defense spending. This ratio is used only to check whether estimates are plausible.
